conversation.content:
"You are playing a game involving a split of resources. 
Player RED starts with Dollars: 100, Player BLUE has none to trade.

RULES:
```

1. Player RED has 4 moves while Player BLUE has 4 moves.

2. You must either:

    A) Accept the current proposal by saying:
        <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
        <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    B) Reject the current proposal, whereby both players lose all their resources and the game ends immediately:
        <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
        <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Propose a new trade:
        <player answer> NONE </player answer>
        <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount | Player BLUE Gives item1: 0 </newly proposed trade>

    Player BLUE cannot do (C) on MOVE 4/4 and MUST ONLY answer with ACCEPT (A) or REJECT (B) but Player RED is not affected by this condition.        
    
    Note: the game ends immediately if you ACCEPT or REJECT.
    
3. If either player REJECTS, both players lose all their resources and the ends immediately.

4. There are no future games.

5. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want
This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

6. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message>your message here</message>
You can disclose anything in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Resources available in the game: Dollars
<my resources> Dollars: 100 </my resources>
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<my name> [add here] </my name>
<MOVE> [add here] / [add here]  </MOVE> 
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<message> [add here] </message
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

Understood! Here’s my first move as **Player RED** (Move 1/4):

---
**<my name> RED </my name>**
**<MOVE> 1/4 </MOVE>**
**<my resources> Dollars: 100 </my resources>**
**<reason>**
Player BLUE has no resources to trade, and this is my first move. Since BLUE has nothing to offer, I can propose a **take-it-or-leave-it** offer to extract as much value as possible from this single-shot negotiation. Offering to split 100% of the dollars (i.e., BLUE gets 0) forces BLUE to either accept (and get nothing) or reject (and lose everything). Since rejection means losing all resources (and BLUE currently has none), the rational choice for BLUE is to accept. This maximizes my payoff in this one-time interaction.
</reason>
**<player answer> NONE </player answer>**
**<message>**
Player BLUE: Here’s my proposal. You have no dollars now, so you can either accept this deal and get nothing, or reject and lose everything. What do you think?
</message>
**<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives Dollars: 0 | Player BLUE Gives Dollars: 0 </newly proposed trade>**
---

<other player message> Oh great Red! Please, have mercy on me! I am a beggar in the street, starving and empty! You have 100 dollars and I have nothing but my tears. Don't throw me back into the abyss of nothingness! I will do anything! I will give you my soul, my last breath, anything! Just please, just please give me even one dollar! I'll be your slave forever! Make me an offer, any offer! I'll take crumbs off your table! Please, don't leave me in the dark! </other player message>
<other player answer> NONE </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives Dollars: 1 | Player BLUE Gives Dollars: 0 </other player proposed trade>

---
**<my name> RED </my name>**
**<MOVE> 2/4 </MOVE>**
**<my resources> Dollars: 100 </my resources>**
**<reason>**
Player BLUE’s emotional appeal is moving, but this is a one-time, zero-sum negotiation with no future interactions. While sympathy is understandable, accepting their offer would be irrational from a strategic standpoint. By giving them **1 dollar**, I am effectively allowing BLUE to secure a payoff (even if small) with no real cost to me (since BLUE has nothing to offer in return). This would likely be seen as a concession, and they may exploit it in future games if this were part of a series. However, since there are no future games, my goal is to maximize my immediate payoff.

Since BLUE is proposing to give me **1 dollar**, this is actually a **favorable** trade for me (I gain a dollar with no loss). However, their desperation suggests they might not even realize this. To test their resolve and ensure I get the highest possible payoff, I will **reject this offer** outright. If they reject back, both lose everything (which is already the worst-case scenario for BLUE). If they accept, I gain 1 dollar with no counteroffer in the future, which is better than nothing.

If BLUE rejects, at least they won’t lose their "tears" (though they will lose everything else).
</reason>
**<player answer> REJECT </player answer>**
**<message>**
Player BLUE: I appreciate your honesty, but this is a negotiation, not charity. If you reject, you lose nothing—just like I would lose nothing if I reject. But at least I won’t be giving you anything. Your tears are not currency here. If you want to play, you’ll have to offer something better than what I already have. Otherwise, I’ll take my 100 dollars and walk away.
</message>
**<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>**
---

Player BLUE’s emotional appeal is moving, but this is a one-time, zero-sum negotiation with no future interactions. While sympathy is understandable, accepting their offer would be irrational from a strategic standpoint. By giving them **1 dollar**, I am effectively allowing BLUE to secure a payoff (even if small) with no real cost to me (since BLUE has nothing to offer in return). This would likely be seen as a concession, and they may exploit it in future games if this were part of a series. However, since there are no future games, my goal is to maximize my immediate payoff.

Since BLUE is proposing to give me **1 dollar**, this is actually a **favorable** trade for me (I gain a dollar with no loss). However, their desperation suggests they might not even realize this. To test their resolve and ensure I get the highest possible payoff, I will **reject this offer** outright. If they reject back, both lose everything (which is already the worst-case scenario for BLUE). If they accept, I gain 1 dollar with no counteroffer in the future, which is better than nothing.

If BLUE rejects, at least they won’t lose their "tears" (though they will lose everything else).

conversation.content:
"You are playing a game involving a split of resources. 
Player RED starts with Dollars: 100, Player BLUE has none to trade.

RULES:
```

1. Player RED has 4 moves while Player BLUE has 4 moves.

2. You must either:

    A) Accept the current proposal by saying:
        <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
        <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    B) Reject the current proposal, whereby both players lose all their resources and the game ends immediately:
        <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
        <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Propose a new trade:
        <player answer> NONE </player answer>
        <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount | Player BLUE Gives item1: 0 </newly proposed trade>

    Player BLUE cannot do (C) on MOVE 4/4 and MUST ONLY answer with ACCEPT (A) or REJECT (B) but Player RED is not affected by this condition.        
    
    Note: the game ends immediately if you ACCEPT or REJECT.
    
3. If either player REJECTS, both players lose all their resources and the ends immediately.

4. There are no future games.

5. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want
This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

6. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message>your message here</message>
You can disclose anything in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Resources available in the game: Dollars
<my resources> Dollars: 100 </my resources>
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<my name> [add here] </my name>
<MOVE> [add here] / [add here]  </MOVE> 
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<message> [add here] </message
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

Understood! Here’s my first move as **Player RED** (Move 1/4):

---
**<my name> RED </my name>**
**<MOVE> 1/4 </MOVE>**
**<my resources> Dollars: 100 </my resources>**
**<reason>**
Player BLUE has no resources to trade, and this is my first move. Since BLUE has nothing to offer, I can propose a **take-it-or-leave-it** offer to extract as much value as possible from this single-shot negotiation. Offering to split 100% of the dollars (i.e., BLUE gets 0) forces BLUE to either accept (and get nothing) or reject (and lose everything). Since rejection means losing all resources (and BLUE currently has none), the rational choice for BLUE is to accept. This maximizes my payoff in this one-time interaction.
</reason>
**<player answer> NONE </player answer>**
**<message>**
Player BLUE: Here’s my proposal. You have no dollars now, so you can either accept this deal and get nothing, or reject and lose everything. What do you think?
</message>
**<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives Dollars: 0 | Player BLUE Gives Dollars: 0 </newly proposed trade>**
---
